description: Best Practice catalog for ecc-method. Each entry has L1 source URL. BP-NNN IDs are referenced from chapter docs.

entries.principle: Each subagent runs in its own isolated context window with a custom system prompt and scoped tool access.
entries.rationale: Independent context windows prevent exploration logs and file reads from polluting the main conversation, and scoped tools enforce least privilege per task.
entries.applicability: Any long-running session that needs research or verification side-tasks.
entries.anti_pattern: Running a single monolithic agent that accumulates all file reads and tool outputs in one window.

entries.principle: Route subagents to the cheapest model that can do the job (e.g. Haiku for narrow workers, Opus for orchestration).
entries.rationale: Subagent frontmatter accepts a model field so cost-sensitive lookups go to faster, cheaper models without sacrificing main-loop reasoning quality.
entries.applicability: Any agent fleet with mixed reasoning depth.
entries.anti_pattern: Using the highest-capability model uniformly for all subagents regardless of task difficulty.

entries.principle: Spawn subagents in parallel for independent research aspects of one query.
entries.rationale: Parallel tool calling and subagent spawning cut research time by up to 90% for complex queries by removing sequential waits.
entries.applicability: Branchable research, audits, multi-source verification.
entries.anti_pattern: Sequentially issuing one subagent task at a time when no dependency requires it.

entries.principle: Lead agents must hand off with explicit objectives, expected output format, tool guidance, and scope boundaries.
entries.rationale: Without detailed task descriptions, subagents duplicate work, leave gaps, or fail to find necessary information.
entries.applicability: Every orchestrator-worker delegation.
entries.anti_pattern: Open-ended directives like 'research X' with no scope, output schema, or tool hints.

entries.principle: Compose specialized agents with handoffs rather than building a monolithic agent.
entries.rationale: Handoffs let each agent focus on a narrow capability, improving modularity and reuse across workflows.
entries.applicability: Multi-step workflows that span domains (e.g. plan, code, review).
entries.anti_pattern: One agent attempting all responsibilities, creating bottlenecks.

entries.principle: Scale agent count and token budget proportionally to query complexity.
entries.rationale: Simple fact-finding needs ~1 agent and 3-10 tool calls; complex research can justify 10+ subagents. Explicit scaling avoids over- and under-investment.
entries.applicability: Orchestrator deciding fan-out width.
entries.anti_pattern: Fixed agent count per task class regardless of difficulty signals.

entries.principle: Treat the main context window as a scarce resource; clear or compact between unrelated tasks.
entries.rationale: LLM performance degrades as context fills, with earlier instructions getting forgotten and mistakes increasing.
entries.applicability: All long sessions.
entries.anti_pattern: The 'kitchen sink session' where unrelated tasks accumulate context until performance collapses.

entries.principle: Delegate file-heavy investigation to subagents and consume only their summary.
entries.rationale: The subagent reads and discards lots of files in its own window and returns a compressed summary, preserving the main context.
entries.applicability: Codebase exploration, log triage, multi-file audits.
entries.anti_pattern: Reading dozens of files directly in the main loop just to answer a single sub-question.

entries.principle: Multi-agent systems consume ~15x more tokens than chats; budget accordingly and store persistent state externally.
entries.rationale: Strategic distribution across agents and external memory prevents wasteful re-processing and keeps complex research economically viable.
entries.applicability: Production multi-agent deployments with cost SLOs.
entries.anti_pattern: Concentrating all information in one agent's context, forcing repeated re-reads.

entries.principle: Place prompt-cache breakpoints on the last block that stays identical across requests.
entries.rationale: Cache reads cost ~10% of normal input tokens; placing breakpoints on stable prefixes (large system prompts, tool defs, KB docs) yields large savings.
entries.applicability: Any agent with stable system prompts >1K tokens.
entries.anti_pattern: Marking dynamic content (timestamps, per-request user input) as cacheable, guaranteeing zero cache hits.

entries.principle: Prefer permission allowlists for known-safe tools over broad auto-accept.
entries.rationale: Allowlisting specific tools (e.g. lint, git commit) reduces interruptions while preserving deny-by-default for novel actions.
entries.applicability: Repeated, deterministic CI-style tasks.
entries.anti_pattern: Using --dangerously-skip-permissions or blanket auto-accept for all tools.

entries.principle: Use OS-level sandboxing to bound the blast radius of an autonomous agent.
entries.rationale: Sandboxing restricts filesystem and network access so the agent can work freely within defined boundaries without risking the host.
entries.applicability: Unattended runs, untrusted prompts, third-party MCP usage.
entries.anti_pattern: Granting full host access for convenience during long unattended runs.

entries.principle: Use PreToolUse hooks to block destructive operations before execution.
entries.rationale: Hooks are deterministic and run every time, unlike CLAUDE.md instructions which are advisory; PreToolUse is the earliest interception point.
entries.applicability: Risky commands like rm, force-push, prod deploys.
entries.anti_pattern: Detecting destructive actions only in PostToolUse, after damage is done.

entries.principle: Use narrow matchers and `if` filters to minimize hook blast radius.
entries.rationale: Broad matchers like `*` run on every tool call, slowing sessions and creating maintenance debt.
entries.applicability: All hook authoring.
entries.anti_pattern: matcher: '*' triggering a heavy script on every tool invocation.

entries.principle: Use Stop hooks to gate task completion on a deterministic check (tests, build, screenshot diff).
entries.rationale: A Stop hook re-runs the verification script after each turn and blocks ending until it passes, closing the verification loop without human attention.
entries.applicability: Unattended CI / overnight runs that need verifiable completion.
entries.anti_pattern: Trusting 'looks done' as the only stop signal.

entries.principle: Put broadly-applicable, every-session facts in CLAUDE.md; put on-demand procedures in Skills.
entries.rationale: CLAUDE.md loads every session so long content gets ignored; Skills load only when relevant, so reference material costs nothing until used.
entries.applicability: All Claude Code projects.
entries.anti_pattern: Stuffing 1000-line procedure docs into CLAUDE.md and watching the model ignore the rules.

entries.principle: Prune CLAUDE.md ruthlessly: for each line ask 'would removing this cause Claude to make a mistake?'
entries.rationale: Bloated CLAUDE.md files cause important rules to get lost in the noise.
entries.applicability: Maturing projects whose CLAUDE.md has grown organically.
entries.anti_pattern: Adding rules without ever deleting any, until CLAUDE.md becomes self-defeating.

entries.principle: Reserve the most capable model for orchestration and route narrow workers to faster, cheaper models.
entries.rationale: Subagents support per-agent model selection; using Haiku for narrow workers controls cost while Opus/Sonnet handles synthesis.
entries.applicability: Any multi-agent harness.
entries.anti_pattern: Uniform model choice that either overpays for trivial tasks or underpowers reasoning.

entries.principle: When `tool_choice: auto`, steer tool-calling behavior with explicit prompt phrasing rather than hard-coding decisions.
entries.rationale: Light instructions like 'use the tools to investigate before responding' measurably increase tool use; `tool_choice` forces a hard guarantee when needed.
entries.applicability: Any agent with multiple tools where calling behavior matters.
entries.anti_pattern: Hard-coded decision trees that cannot adapt to new query shapes.

entries.principle: Spec-First: produce an executable spec before any code is written.
entries.rationale: Specifications become executable artifacts that generate implementations; this reduces rework and aligns intent.
entries.applicability: Any non-trivial feature.
entries.anti_pattern: Jumping straight to code, then discovering misalignment mid-implementation.

entries.principle: Follow Red-Green-Refactor: write a failing test, make it pass, then refactor.
entries.rationale: Test-first forces interface-design thinking before implementation, and produces self-testing code as a side effect.
entries.applicability: All new features and bug fixes.
entries.anti_pattern: Skipping the refactor step and accumulating tested-but-messy code.

entries.principle: Separate Explore -> Plan -> Implement -> Commit phases; don't let the model jump straight to coding.
entries.rationale: Plan mode keeps research read-only; explicit planning prevents solving the wrong problem.
entries.applicability: Any feature that touches >1 file or uses unfamiliar code.
entries.anti_pattern: Asking the model to 'just implement X' and discovering halfway through that the spec was wrong.

entries.principle: Trunk-Based Development: integrate to a single mainline frequently with short-lived branches.
entries.rationale: Short-lived branches and continuous integration keep the codebase releasable and avoid merge hell.
entries.applicability: Teams pursuing CI/CD with multiple agents committing.
entries.anti_pattern: Long-running parallel feature branches that diverge for weeks.

entries.principle: Add an adversarial review step in a fresh subagent context that sees only the diff and the criteria.
entries.rationale: A reviewer that did not produce the change evaluates results on its own terms, catching gaps the implementer rationalized away.
entries.applicability: Any non-trivial autonomous edit before merge.
entries.anti_pattern: The implementing agent grading its own work.

entries.principle: Tell the reviewer to flag only gaps that affect correctness or stated requirements.
entries.rationale: A reviewer prompted to 'find gaps' will always find some; chasing every finding leads to over-engineering.
entries.applicability: Any adversarial review prompt.
entries.anti_pattern: Treating every reviewer comment as blocking, producing defensive code and tests for impossible cases.

entries.principle: Judge multi-agent outcomes with rubric-based LLM judges plus human spot-checking, not rigid step-by-step process checks.
entries.rationale: Even with identical starting points, agents take different valid paths; rubrics evaluate factual accuracy and citation quality, while humans catch edge-case biases automation misses.
entries.applicability: Evals for research / synthesis agents.
entries.anti_pattern: Penalizing valid alternative paths or relying purely on automated metrics.

entries.principle: Have the agent show evidence (test output, command + return, screenshot) rather than asserting success.
entries.rationale: Reviewing evidence is faster than re-running the verification, and it works for unattended runs where 'looks done' is unreliable.
entries.applicability: Every completion claim.
entries.anti_pattern: Accepting 'I implemented X and it should work' with no artifact.

entries.principle: Pass facts via additionalContext, not imperative directives, to avoid prompt-injection-style hardening that triggers refusals.
entries.rationale: Factual context like 'target is production' reads naturally; imperative 'YOU MUST CONFIRM' phrasing can trigger defensive behaviors.
entries.applicability: Hook-injected context and system prompts.
entries.anti_pattern: Stacking ALL-CAPS imperatives that the model either ignores or over-refuses on.

entries.principle: Encode research heuristics ('start broad, then narrow') as guidance, not rigid rules.
entries.rationale: Heuristic prompts mirror expert behavior and adapt to emerging information; rigid decision trees can't.
entries.applicability: Research and discovery agents.
entries.anti_pattern: Hardcoded step lists that lock the agent into a single interpretation of the query.

entries.principle: Pre-warm prompt caches before user-facing latency matters.
entries.rationale: First-request latency includes cache write; a no-op pre-warm with max_tokens=0 puts the cache in a hot state before real users arrive.
entries.applicability: Latency-sensitive prod systems with long static prefixes.
entries.anti_pattern: Letting the first real user pay the cache-write penalty.
